frameworks.name: canonical cortical microcircuit
frameworks.one_line: input arrives in layer 4; output and feedback occupy superficial and deep layers

frameworks.name: top-down attentional modulation
frameworks.one_line: feedback signals selectively bias sensory processing across cortical layers

core_question: Do bottom-up sensory and top-down attentional signals occupy dissociable cortical layers in human primary visual cortex?
key_assumptions: laminar fMRI resolves activity within distinct cortical layers of V1
BOLD signal in input layer 4 reflects feedforward sensory drive
BOLD signal in superficial and deep layers reflects feedback or output processing
manipulations: bottom-up sensory drive via oriented gratings
top-down attentional state during orientation-discrimination task
cortical depth (layer) as the spatial readout dimension
key_findings: bottom-up sensory-evoked responses peak in middle (input) layers of V1
top-down attention modulation is strongest in superficial and deep layers
the two signal types occupy dissociable laminar profiles within the same V1 voxels
interpretations: feedforward input and feedback attentional modulation are anatomically separable at the laminar scale in human V1
laminar fMRI can dissociate canonical input vs output/feedback compartments of cortical computation
